Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky.event: Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky born in Dashkovichy, Grodno Governorate in the Russian Empire (now Belarus). The village was so small that there was no minyan on Shabbos and the nearest Kehillah was outside the T'chum in Mosty. His family would camp out in the Mosty shul floor for Yamim Noraim with other Jews from nearby vilages.

Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky attend Cheder in Mosty walking there and back. By the time he was 10 he was already familiar with the 24 books of Tanach. He would recite entire chapters of Tanach to local Jews. At the age of 10, his father hires a teacher for Gemara. He studies on his own after his Barmizvah.

R'Yoel Baranchik, a Talmid of the Alter of Novardok, came to Dashkovichy and persuades the family of Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky to send him to Yeshivah in Navardok. He later reported that R'Baranchik was sent by the Alter who had heard of Dayan Abramsky. Dayan Abramsky when davening in the Shul of Novadok met the Aruch HaShulchan, who was so impressed that he gave him Smicha. He also suggested to his cousin that their daughter would make a suitable wife for him.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky goes to learn in Telz and is Charvuta with R'Kahaneman.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky, is forced to leave Telz as he is of age to join the Russian army. He goes to study under R'Chaim Ozer of Grodzinsky in Vilna.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky is pursuaded by his father in law to study in Brisk and meets R'Chaim Soloveitchik from whom he learns not only Gemara but also how to handle communal affairs.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky becomes Rav of Smol'yany.

Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky becomes Rav of Smalyavichy. During this time, R'Chaim Soloveitchik was forced to leave with the Jews of Brisk and they settled temporarily in Minsk and he would spend much of his week with R'Chaim in Minsk. He also had a close freindship with the Brisker Rav. The town of Smalyavichy changes hands several times.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky takes over as Rav of Slutsk from R'Ahron Kotler. But the Stalin grip increased and the communists closed the Shul and the Yeshivah and confiscated the buildings.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky offered a position as Rav of Petach Tikvah but was prevented from leaving by the communists.

Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky with R'Shlomo Zevin creates a Torah Journal called Yagdil Torah for Rabbanim in Russia to give chizuk and answer questions. This was tremendously couragous considering there was no Yeshivah, Shul of Mikveh in the Soviet Union.

40 prominent Rabbanim are arrested in Minsk, 11 were released after strong international pressure and them signing that there was no oppression by the Soviets in a letter that was sent around the world. Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky goes to Moscow to try and get a visa to leave. He finds a place on the outskirts and he quietly hosted refugees there. Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky is nearly arrested and goes to Leningrad and goes into hiding but returns to Moscow and is arrested with his wife. He is beaten and tortured to try and get a confession out of him. He nearly has the death sentence and was sent to Siberia in a labour camp.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky is forced to work in a Labour camp in Siberia.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky is released from Siberia and becomes Rav of Machzikei Hadas in the East End.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky becomes Av Bet Din of the London Bet Din.
Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky moves to Bayit Vegan and teaches in Slobodka Yeshiva Bnei Brak once a week..

Alter of Liadi.event: Became disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch
Died in Piena, Russia, while fleeing Napoleon's invasion

Arizal.event: Born in Jerusalem to an Ashkenazi father and Sephardi mother.
After his father's death, moved with his mother to Cairo, Egypt, where he was raised by his wealthy uncle, Mordechai Francis.
Studied Torah and Kabbalah in Egypt under Rabbi David ibn Abi Zimra (Radbaz) and Rabbi Bezalel Ashkenazi (author of Shita Mekubetzet).
Moved to Safed in the Galilee and quickly became the leading Kabbalist, attracting disciples such as Rabbi Chaim Vital, who recorded his teachings.
Died in Safed at the young age of 38, leaving behind a spiritual revolution in Jewish mysticism.

Aruch HaShulchan.event: Born in Bobruisk, near Vilna, in the Russian Empire (now Belarus).
Around this time, R'Yechiel Michel Epstein goes to study in Volozhin and stays there for two years. Married the sister of Rav Naftali Zvi Yehuda Berlin (the Netziv).
R'Yechiel Michel Epstein returns to Bobruisk and supports the Rav there.
R'Yechiel Michel Epstein was appointed Rav of Novozybkhov. This town had a large number of Chasidish Jews.
Appointed rabbi of Novogrudok, where he served for over three decades and composed his magnum opus 'Aruch HaShulchan'.
Died in Novogrudok. His 'Aruch HaShulchan' became a cornerstone of halakhic study and is widely used until today.

Abarbanel.event: Born in Lisbon, Portugal, into a wealthy and influential Jewish family.
Fled to Toledo, Spain, after being accused of treason in Portugal. Became financial advisor to King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella.
Expelled with Spanish Jewry during the Alhambra Decree. Tried unsuccessfully to prevent the expulsion by offering the crown a ransom.
Settled in Naples, Italy, where he began writing his major biblical commentaries.
Moved to Venice, where he advised the Venetian Republic and continued writing.
Died in Venice. Buried in Padua. His commentaries and messianic writings influenced generations.

Baal Shem Tov.event: Baal Shem Tov is born, likely in the Podolia region (traditions place his birth near Okopy, close to Kamianets-Podilskyi).
Baal Shem Tov settles in Mezhibuz (modern-day Medzhybizh, Ukraine), where he begins to gather disciples and spread his teachings.
Baal Shem Tov leads a circle of disciples, including Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye and Rabbi Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezritch, who would become key leaders of early Hasidism.
Baal Shem Tov passes away on Shavuot (6 Sivan 5510 / May 21, 1760) in Mezhibuz, and is buried in the Jewish cemetery there.

ישראל אבוחצירא (Baba Sali).event: Born in Tafilalt, Morocco, into the illustrious Abuhatzeira rabbinic dynasty.
Began serving as a rabbinic leader in Morocco; became known for Torah scholarship and ascetic lifestyle.
Immigrated to Israel; initially settled in Jerusalem.
Moved to Netivot in the Negev; became famous as a miracle worker and spiritual guide for Jews across Israel.
Passed away in Netivot; his funeral drew hundreds of thousands, and his tomb became a major pilgrimage site.

יואל סירקיש (Bach).event: Born in Kraków, Poland, into a scholarly rabbinic family.
Served as rabbi of Belz, where he established his reputation as a leading halakhist.
Appointed rabbi of Brest-Litovsk (Brisk), a major Jewish center in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Became rabbi of Kraków, one of the most prestigious posts in Poland.
Died in Kraków. Buried in the Old Jewish Cemetery. His Bayit Chadash became a cornerstone of halakhic study.

Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk (Baal She.event: Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk is born, possibly in Fürth, Bavaria (alternative tradition: Pidhaytsi, Podolia).
Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk demonstrates kabbalistic powers at the castle of Count von Rantzau in Holzminden after narrowly escaping execution in Westphalia.
Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk settles in London, residing at Prescott Street and later Wellclose Square, becoming known as the Baal Shem of London.
Folklore recounts that Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk saved the Great Synagogue of London from fire by inscribing Hebrew words on its pillars.
Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk dies in London on 17 April 1782 and is buried in Alderney Road Cemetery, Mile End.

Haham Isaac Nieto.event: Isaac Nieto is born in London, the son of Rabbi David Nieto.
Isaac Nieto is appointed Haham of the Portuguese congregation Sha'are Hashamayim (Bevis Marks) in London.
Isaac Nieto resigns his post as Haham and leaves London for several years.
Isaac Nieto travels to Gibraltar and establishes the Shaar Hashamayim congregation, becoming Gibraltar's first rabbi.
Isaac Nieto returns to London and is appointed Av Bet Din, serving alongside Rabbis Isaac de Valle and Jacob Coronel.
Isaac Nieto resigns after disputes in the Bet Din and is barred from acting as assessor.

Chacham Tzvi (Rabbi Zvi Ashkenazi).event: Born in Moravia (probably Vienna or nearby)
Served as Chief Rabbi of Amsterdam

החלבן (Rav Yaakov Moshe Hillel).event: Studied Torah and Kabbalah under prominent Jerusalem rabbis.
Founded Yeshivat Ahavat Shalom, dedicated to Torah and Kabbalah.
Became known globally as 'HaChalban', attracting thousands of students and followers.

R' Avraham Yeshaya Karelitz.event: Chazon Ish born in Kossow, near Grodno (present-day Belarus). His youth is spent with private teachers and individual study apart from a few unsuccessful stints in Yeshivah.
Chazon Ish spends a short stint in Vilna studying under R' Chaim Ozer Grodzinski.
Chazon Ish marries and settles in Kovidan, where his wife opens a fabric shop. He writes this period was his golden period of uninterrupted Torah study. His chavruta was R' Moshe Rozin
Chazon Ish with many other Jews flee from the battle areas and settle in Soybtz.
Chazon Ish moves to Minsk with his wife following soon after.
Chazon Ish immigrates to Eretz Yisrael; settles in Bnei Brak, becoming its spiritual leader.
Chazon Ish passes away in Bnei Brak; tens of thousands attend his funeral. Buried in Bnei Brak, revered as the guiding spirit of Torah Jewry in Israel.

Haim Yosef David Azulai (Hida).event: Haim Yosef David Azulai (Hida) is born in Jerusalem into the illustrious Azulai rabbinic family.
Hida begins his first emissary journey from Hebron, traveling through Italy, Germany, and Western Europe to raise funds and strengthen Jewish communities.
Hida visits Germany, meeting leading rabbis including the Pnei Yehoshua.
Hida embarks on his second emissary journey, traveling through Tunisia, Morocco, Italy, France, and Holland.
Hida publishes his seminal works Shem HaGedolim and Birkei Yosef in Livorno and Pisa, cementing his scholarly reputation.
Hida marries his second wife Rachel in Pisa and settles permanently in Livorno, Italy.
Hida dies in Livorno after decades of publishing, study, and leadership. He is buried there.
Hida's remains are reinterred on Har HaMenuchot in Jerusalem, fulfilling his final wish.

Yisrael Meir ha-Kohen Kagan (Chofetz Cha.location: Dzyatlava, Grodno Governorate, Russian Empire (now Belarus)

Yisrael Meir ha-Kohen Kagan (Chofetz Cha.event: Moved for education after father's passing

Dayan Chanoch Ehrentreu.event: Born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany, into a rabbinic family.
Family emigrated to the UK; his father became principal of Prestwich Jewish Day School in Manchester.
Attended Hasmonean High School in London.
Studied at Gateshead Yeshiva, becoming a prominent Talmudic scholar.
Founded the Sunderland Kollel and served as its head until 1979.
Became Av Beth Din and Communal Rabbi of Manchester.
Appointed Rosh Beth Din of London by Chief Rabbi Lord Jakobovits.
Appointed Rabbi Yonason Abraham to the London Beth Din.
Oversaw the successful construction of the North-West London eruv, despite controversy.
Barred Rabbi Dr. Louis Jacobs from taking part in a synagogue service, sparking debate.
Retired as head of the London Beth Din.
Became Av Beth Din of the European Beth Din in Basel, Switzerland.
Died in London at the age of 89.

Rabbi Jonathan Eybeschutz.event: Born in Cracow (disputed, some say Prague)
Became Chief Rabbi of Altona, Hamburg, and Wandsbek

Rabbi Eliyahu ben Shlomo Zalman (Vilna G.event: Rabbi Eliyahu ben Shlomo Zalman, later known as the Vilna Gaon, is born in Sialiec near Brisk, in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.
The Vilna Gaon settles permanently in Vilna, dedicating his life to Torah study while declining formal rabbinic office.
The Vilna Gaon signs the first excommunication (cherem) against the Hasidic movement, marking the start of organized opposition (Mitnagdim).
The Vilna Gaon passes away in Vilna on 19 Tishrei 5558 (October 9, 1797). He is buried in the Vilna cemetery.

Hermann Adler HaKohen.event: Born in Hanover, Germany, to Chief Rabbi Nathan Marcus Adler.
Studied at the rabbinical college in Prague.
Graduated PhD from University of Leipzig.
Received semikha (ordination) from Rabbi Solomon Judah Loeb Rapoport.
Appointed minister of Bayswater Synagogue, London.
Became assistant to his father, Chief Rabbi Nathan Marcus Adler.
Succeeded his father as Chief Rabbi of the British Empire.
Consecrated Dublin Hebrew Congregation synagogue; declared Ireland had never persecuted Jews.
Appointed Commander of the Royal Victorian Order (CVO).
Died and was buried at Willesden United Synagogue Cemetery, London.

ר׳ אברהם מרדכי אלתר (Imrei Emes).event: Born in Góra Kalwaria (Ger), Poland, son of the Sfas Emes (Rav Yehudah Aryeh Leib Alter).
Succeeded his father, the Sfas Emes, as the Gerrer Rebbe, attracting tens of thousands of chasidim.
Led Ger through World War I; many chasidim displaced and Ger institutions suffered, but the Rebbe rebuilt the community.
Escaped Nazi-occupied Poland at the outbreak of World War II, fleeing through Lithuania and eventually reaching Palestine.
Settled in Jerusalem, reestablishing Ger Hasidut in Eretz Yisrael and leading Holocaust survivors.
Passed away in Jerusalem during Israel’s War of Independence; tens of thousands attended his funeral.

Immanuel Jakobovits.note: Born in Königsberg (then East Prussia, now Kaliningrad, Russia)
Family moved to Berlin; father became rabbinical judge
Fled Nazi Germany; studied in London, received semicha
Rabbi of Fifth Avenue Synagogue, New York
Became Chief Rabbi of the United Hebrew Congregations of the Commonwealth
Died in London; buried on Mount of Olives, Jerusalem

Rabbi Levi Yitzchok of Berditchev (Kedus.event: Rabbi Levi Yitzchok is born in Husakiv (Yavoriv Raion, Ukraine) to Rabbi Meir and Sarah-Sasha Ruskov.
Rabbi Levi Yitzchok marries Perel, daughter of Rabbi Israel Peretz of Levertov, and soon becomes a disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch.
Rabbi Levi Yitzchok serves as rabbi in Żelechów, Pinsk, and other towns, gaining fame as a compassionate Hasidic leader.
Rabbi Levi Yitzchok settles in Berdichev, where he serves as rabbi and becomes renowned as the 'defense attorney of Israel.'
Kedushas Levi, his magnum opus commentary on Torah and Jewish holidays, is published for the first time.
Rabbi Levi Yitzchok of Berditchev passes away on 25 Tishrei, 5570 (October 5, 1809) and is buried in Berdichev’s old Jewish cemetery.

Rabbi Aryeh Leib Kahana Heller (Ketzos H.event: Rabbi Aryeh Leib Heller is born in Kalush, Galicia (today Ukraine), into a distinguished rabbinic family.
Rabbi Aryeh Leib Heller studies Torah under Rabbi Meshulam Igra of Tysmienica, becoming known as a prodigy in Talmud and halacha.
Ketzos HaChoshen is published, a halachic masterpiece on Choshen Mishpat that becomes a cornerstone of Talmudic study.
Rabbi Aryeh Leib Heller becomes rabbi of Stryi, Galicia, where he serves until his death in 1812.
Meshoveiv Nesivos is written in response to Rabbi Yaakov Lorberbaum’s 'Netivot ha-Mishpat,' continuing their halachic dialogue.
Rabbi Aryeh Leib Heller passes away in Stryi, remembered as one of Galicia’s greatest halachic authorities.

ר' אברהם די בוטון (Abraham de Boton, Lec.event: Born in Salonika, an important center of Sephardic Jewry in the Ottoman Empire.
Became a leading figure in the Salonika rabbinate; began writing Lechem Mishneh.
Moved to Adrianople (Edirne), where he established and led a major yeshiva.
Passed away in Adrianople; his Lechem Mishneh soon became a standard commentary on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah.

מרדכי יפה (Mordechai Yoffe, the Levush).event: Born in Prague or possibly Grodno (sources differ), into a learned Ashkenazi family.
Studied under leading rabbis of his time, including the Rema (Rabbi Moshe Isserles) and the Maharshal (Rabbi Shlomo Luria).
Served as rabbi of Grodno, gaining reputation as a halakhic decisor and communal leader.
Became rabbi of Lublin, one of the major centers of Torah learning in Poland.
Served as rabbi and dayan in Prague; began work on his monumental Levushim.
Completed and began publishing the Levushim, a ten-volume halakhic code harmonizing the Shulchan Aruch with Ashkenazic custom.
Died in Prague, leaving behind his influential halakhic code, which was widely studied in Europe.

Menachem Mendel Schneerson.event: Born in Nikolaev (Mykolaiv), Russian Empire, eldest son of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson and Rebbetzin Chana.
Moved with family to Yekaterinoslav (now Dnipro), where his father became Chief Rabbi.
Visited Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn (the sixth Rebbe) in Riga, meeting his future wife, Chaya Mushka.
Married Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka in Warsaw, Poland, with leading rabbis in attendance.
Moved to Berlin; studied mathematics, physics, and philosophy at the University of Berlin.
After the Nazi rise to power, relocated to Paris and studied engineering at the Sorbonne.
Escaped Nazi-occupied Europe via Lisbon and arrived in New York on June 23. Began leading Chabad’s central institutions.
On 10 Shevat 5711, officially accepted leadership as the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe at 770 Eastern Parkway, Brooklyn.
Launched massive global Jewish outreach, establishing schools, synagogues, and mitzvah campaigns such as tefillin and Shabbat candles.
Suffered a major heart attack during Simchat Torah at 770. Chose to be treated there instead of at a hospital, later recovering.
U.S. Congress and President Jimmy Carter declared his birthday, 11 Nisan, as 'Education Day USA.'
Began the custom of distributing 'Sunday Dollars' for charity, greeting and blessing thousands weekly.
Suffered a severe stroke, leaving him unable to speak and partially paralyzed.
Passed away on June 12 (3 Tammuz 5754) in Brooklyn. Buried at the Ohel in Queens beside his father-in-law, the sixth Rebbe.
Posthumously awarded the Congressional Gold Medal. His global network of shluchim grew to over 5,000 institutions.

ר' וידאל די טולוסה (Vidal of Tolosa, Mag.event: Born in Tolosa or possibly in Barcelona, in the Crown of Aragon.
Studied Torah and halakha in the Spanish yeshivot, in the intellectual climate of the Rishonim.
Began writing his Maggid Mishneh commentary on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah, clarifying sources and addressing critiques.
Passed away in Spain; his Maggid Mishneh became foundational for later study and psak.

Rabbi Dov Ber ben Avraham of Mezeritch (.event: Dov Ber ben Avraham is born in Lokachi, Volhynia (approximate date and place).
Dov Ber becomes a close disciple of Rabbi Israel Baal Shem Tov in Medzhybizh, abandoning his ascetic practices.
Dov Ber assumes leadership of the Hasidic movement after the passing of the Baal Shem Tov, establishing his court in Mezeritch.
Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezeritch, passes away on 15 December 1772 and is buried in Hannopil beside Rabbi Zusha of Hanipol.

יהודה ליווא בן בצלאל (Maharal of Prague).event: Born in Poznań (or possibly Worms); later associated with Moravia and Prague.
Served as rabbi of Moravia, residing in various towns.
Appointed chief rabbi of Moravia, centered in Mikulov.
Became rabbi of Prague; established yeshivah and exerted strong communal leadership.
Led Prague community; legendary association with the creation of the Golem.
Visited Emperor Rudolf II in Prague; noted for philosophical and mystical discussions.
Returned to Prague rabbinate after disputes; continued teaching and writing.
Died in Prague; buried in the Old Jewish Cemetery, where his grave remains a pilgrimage site.

מאיר בן ברוך מרוטנבורג (Maharam of Rothe.event: Born in Worms, part of medieval Germany, into a distinguished rabbinic family.
Studied under Rabbi Yechiel of Paris and other Tosafists in France; influenced by the Paris Talmud disputation (1240).
Returned to Germany, teaching and serving as rabbi in various communities, including Rothenburg, Wurzburg, and Mainz.
Attempted to emigrate to Eretz Yisrael with many followers, but was captured and imprisoned by Emperor Rudolf I near Ensisheim.
Died in prison in Ensisheim after seven years of captivity. His body was held for ransom and only buried years later in Worms by Alexander ben Shlomo Wimpfen, who paid the ransom.

שמואל אליעזר איידלס (Maharsha).event: Born in Kraków, Poland, into a rabbinic family.
Served as rabbi and opened a yeshivah in Lwów (Lviv).
Became rabbi and rosh yeshivah in Lublin; gained fame as a leading Talmudist.
Appointed rabbi in Tykocin and later Ostroh; continued leading large yeshivot.
Served as rabbi in Kiev, contributing to Jewish scholarship in Eastern Europe.
Became rabbi of Ostroh; wrote and refined his Talmudic commentaries.
Died in Ostroh. Buried there; remembered as one of the greatest Talmudic commentators.

שלמה לוריא (Maharshal).event: Born in Brisk (Brest-Litovsk), in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Studied in Poland, possibly Warsaw and nearby communities.
Appointed Rosh Yeshivah in Lublin; became renowned for his rigorous Talmudic method.
Led one of the largest yeshivot in Poland; trained students who became leaders across Europe.
Died in Lublin; buried in the Old Jewish Cemetery of Lublin, near the Maharam of Lublin.

שמואל מרגליות (Shmuel Margolies).event: Born in Galicia (then part of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth).
Immigrated to New York; became rabbi of Congregation Shearith Israel, the first synagogue in America.
Became recognized as the leading halachic authority in the American colonies.
Died in New York; left a legacy as one of the earliest rabbinic figures in American Jewish history.

Meir Simcha HaKohen of Dvinsk.event: Born in Butrimonys (Baltrimantz), Vilna Governorate, Russian Empire (now Lithuania).
Married at age 17 and moved to Białystok, supported by his wife while he continued Torah study.
Became Rabbi of the non-Hasidic community in Dvinsk (Daugavpils, Latvia), serving 39 years.
Helped expose Shlomo Friedlander’s forged 'lost' tractates of the Jerusalem Talmud, proving them fabrications.
Participated in founding meetings of Agudath Yisrael in Bad Homburg, Germany.
Published 'Ohr Somayach' on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah, which gained wide acclaim.
Opposed secular Zionism but supported Religious Zionism after the Balfour Declaration, declaring the 'Three Oaths' no longer applied.
Died in Riga while seeking medical treatment. Buried in Daugavpils. Left no living descendants.

Chaim Elazar Spira.event: Born in Strzyżów, Galicia, Austria-Hungary, into the Spira rabbinic family.
Appointed Chief Justice of the Rabbinical Court in Munkács, serving alongside his father Rabbi Tzvi Hersh Spira.
Succeeded his father as Chief Rabbi of Munkács and the surrounding region.
Founded the 'Darchei Teshuva' yeshiva and Machzike Torah schools in Munkács.
Traveled to Mandatory Palestine; met privately with the kabbalist Rabbi Solomon Eliezer Alfandari for a week.
His daughter Chaya Fruma Rivka married Rabbi Baruch Yehoshua Yerachmiel Rabinowicz in a celebrated wedding attended by over 20,000 people.
Passed away on May 13 in Munkács; succeeded by his son-in-law Rabbi Baruch Yehoshua Yerachmiel Rabinowicz.

Naftali Zvi Yehuda Berlin (Netziv).note: Born in Mir, Belarus (then Russian Empire)
Began studies at Volozhin Yeshiva at age 11
Betrothed to Reina-Batya, daughter of R' Yitzhak of Volozhin
Began delivering lectures at Volozhin Yeshiva
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Volozhin after passing of R' Eliezer Yitzchak
Participated in Druskininkai Conference; appointed advisory trustee of Chovevei Zion
Closure of Volozhin Yeshiva under Russian government pressure
Died in Warsaw; buried in the Jewish Cemetery

Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum of Lizhensk (No.event: Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum is born in Tyczyn, near Rzeszów, Poland.
Rabbi Elimelech becomes a disciple of Rabbi Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezeritch, and joins the Chevraya Kadisha (Holy Society).
Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum settles in Leżajsk and begins spreading Hasidism throughout Poland-Galicia, attracting many disciples.
Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum passes away on 21 Adar (March 11, 1787) in Leżajsk and is buried there. His grave becomes a major pilgrimage site.

יחזקאל לנדא (Yechezkel Landau, Noda B'Ye.event: Born in Opatów, Poland, into a rabbinic family.
Became dayan (rabbinical judge) and later rabbi in Jampol (Ukraine).
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Prague, leading one of the most important Jewish communities in Europe.
Published the first volume of 'Noda B’Yehuda,' cementing his reputation as a leading posek (halakhic authority).
Passed away in Prague; buried in the Old Jewish Cemetery. His rulings continue to shape halakhic discourse.

Rabbi Chaim ibn Attar (Ohr HaChaim).event: Settled in Jerusalem, founded a yeshiva

Kalonymus Kalman Szapiro.event: Born in Grodzisk Mazowiecki, Poland (19 Iyar 5649), into the Grodzhisk Hasidic dynasty.
Married Rachel Chaya Miriam of Kozhnitz; they had two children, later killed in the Holocaust.
Appointed Rabbi of Piaseczno near Warsaw, where he began to gather Hasidim.
Founded the Da’as Moshe yeshiva in Warsaw, one of the city’s largest between the wars.
Published 'Chovas HaTalmidim,' a guide for youth in Torah study and spiritual growth.
Family tragedy: his only son, daughter-in-law, and sister-in-law killed in Nazi bombing of Warsaw. Soon after, he was confined to the Warsaw Ghetto.
Led clandestine Jewish life in the ghetto: synagogue, mikveh, kosher marriages, and encouragement of Hasidim.
Began delivering Torah discourses on suffering and faith under Nazi persecution, later known as 'Esh Kodesh.'
Buried his writings, including 'Esh Kodesh,' in a milk canister as part of the Oneg Shabbat Archive.
Deported to Trawniki labor camp after Warsaw Ghetto uprising; murdered November 3 during Aktion Erntefest.
'Esh Kodesh,' his ghetto sermons, published in Jerusalem and became a cornerstone of Holocaust-era Torah.

אהרן קוטלר (Aharon Kotler).event: Born in Slutsk, near Minsk (then Russian Empire, now Belarus).
Entered the Slabodka Yeshiva in Kaunas, where he studied under the Alter of Slabodka and Rav Moshe Mordechai Epstein.
Became rosh yeshiva of the Yeshiva of Kletsk in Poland, building it into a major Lithuanian Torah center.
Escaped Nazi-occupied Europe via Japan and arrived in the United States, where he became a leader of American Orthodox Jewry.
Founded Beth Medrash Govoha in Lakewood, New Jersey, which grew to become one of the largest yeshivas in the world.
Co-founded Chinuch Atzmai, the independent Torah school network in Israel, to strengthen religious education.
Died in Lakewood, New Jersey, and was buried in Jerusalem. Left a worldwide legacy of yeshiva learning and Torah institutions.

Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart.event: Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart is born in Breslau, Silesia (modern-day Wrocław, Poland).
Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart is appointed rabbi of the first Ashkenazic synagogue in London, the Great Synagogue at Duke’s Place.
Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart publishes Urim ve-Tummim, the first book printed entirely in Hebrew in London.
Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart passes away in London after serving as chief rabbi of the Ashkenazi community for over 50 years.

Rabbi Aharon Perlov of Karlin (the Great.event: Rabbi Aharon Perlov is born in Yanova to Rabbi Yaakov of Kobrin and Perel.
Rabbi Aharon becomes a close disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch and establishes the Hasidic center in Karlin (today part of Pinsk, Belarus).
Rabbi Aharon, with Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk, spreads Hasidism throughout Lithuania, becoming one of its foremost leaders.
Rabbi Aharon authors the famous Hasidic piyyut Ya Aksuf, later sung in many Hasidic courts on Shabbat.
Rabbi Aharon Perlov of Karlin passes away at the age of 36. His disciple Rabbi Shlomo of Karlin succeeds him and raises his son Rabbi Asher of Stolin, continuing the Karlin-Stolin dynasty.

Rabbi Yehuda Amital.event: Born Yehuda Klein in Oradea, Romania.
Family deported to Auschwitz and killed; Yehuda survived in a Nazi labor camp until liberated by the Soviet Army.
After liberation, traveled to Bucharest before immigrating to Palestine.
Arrived in Palestine; detained briefly in Atlit camp, then settled in Jerusalem. Studied at Hebron Yeshiva under Rabbi Isser Zalman Meltzer, later marrying his granddaughter Miriam.
Joined the Haganah and served in the 1948 War of Independence, fighting in Latrun and the western Galilee.
Served as rabbinic secretary in the Beth Din of Rehovot and later taught at Yeshivat HaDarom, where he began formulating the idea of the Hesder Yeshiva.
Founded Yeshivat Har Etzion in Kfar Etzion after the Six-Day War, with an initial 30 students.
Invited Rabbi Aharon Lichtenstein to join him as co-Rosh Yeshiva of Har Etzion.
Founded the dovish religious Zionist party Meimad, later serving as its chairman.
Appointed minister without portfolio in Shimon Peres’s government after the assassination of Yitzhak Rabin.
The Hesder Yeshiva program, pioneered by Amital, awarded the Israel Prize for special contribution to society and the State of Israel.
Rabbis Yaakov Medan and Baruch Gigi appointed as successors alongside Amital and Lichtenstein.
Retired as Rosh Yeshiva of Har Etzion; Mosheh Lichtenstein became the fourth Rosh Yeshiva.
Passed away in Jerusalem at age 85; buried at Har HaMenuchot, with thousands attending his funeral.

ר׳ אהרן יהודה לייב שטיינמן (Aharon Yehud.event: Born in Brisk (Brest-Litovsk), then part of the Russian Empire (now Belarus).
Studied in the Ponevezh and Grodno yeshivot; became close to the Brisker Rav and other Lithuanian gedolim.
Married Tamar Kornfeld, daughter of Rav Shmuel Kornfeld of Kovno.
Immigrated to Eretz Yisrael after surviving World War II; initially lived in Petah Tikva.
Became Rosh Yeshiva of Yeshivat Gaon Yaakov in Bnei Brak, teaching thousands of talmidim over decades.
After the passing of Rav Elazar Menachem Shach and later Rav Yosef Shalom Elyashiv, recognized as the leading posek and guide of the Haredi world.
Passed away in Bnei Brak at the age of 103. Hundreds of thousands attended his funeral.

אריה קפלן (Aryeh Kaplan).event: Born in New York City, USA.
Studied physics at university level before turning fully to Torah scholarship.
Studied in Torah Vodaath and Mir Yeshiva in Brooklyn, receiving semicha and delving into Jewish mysticism.
Began writing extensively, producing works on Jewish philosophy, Kabbalah, and halakha.
Published his most influential works, including 'Jewish Meditation' and translations of mystical texts.
Passed away suddenly at the age of 48; left a vast literary legacy.

ר׳ אשר אריאלי (Asher Arieli).event: Born in Jerusalem into a rabbinic family.
Studied in Jerusalem yeshivot, showing outstanding brilliance in Talmud study from youth.
Joined the Mir Yeshiva in Jerusalem; became a close disciple of Rav Nosson Tzvi Finkel and Rav Chaim Shmuelevitz.
Began delivering the main shiur klali (general lecture) in Mir Yeshiva, attracting hundreds of talmidim daily.
His shiur grew to over 1,000 students daily, becoming the largest regular shiur in the world.
Continues to give the central shiur at the Mir Yeshiva, influencing thousands of students in the Torah world.

אשר וייס (Asher Weiss).event: Born in Galicia, Poland, into a family of Sanzer chassidim, Holocaust survivors.
Immigrated to Israel with his family; studied Torah intensively in Jerusalem.
Became Rosh Kollel of Machon Minchas Asher; began to gain recognition as a leading posek.
Appointed posek of Shaarei Tzedek Medical Center; issued rulings on complex medical-halakhic questions.
Published multiple volumes of Minchas Asher on Torah, halakha, and responsa; became a global halakhic authority.

Rabbi Baruch HaLevi Epstein.event: Born in Novogrudok to Rabbi Yechiel Michel Epstein (Aruch HaShulchan) and Michla, sister of the Netziv.
At age 13 began studies at the Volozhin Yeshiva, where he was considered a prodigy; studied for five years.
Married the daughter of Rabbi Elazar Moshe HaLevi Horowitz, rabbi of Pinsk, and settled there; studied Torah and general sciences in his father-in-law’s home.
Studied at the Academy of Commerce in Vienna, broadening his education.
Returned to Pinsk, worked as chief bookkeeper and later bank manager; continued writing and study of Torah.
Traveled to the United States to publish and distribute his writings; remained until 1926 before returning to Pinsk.
Died in Pinsk during the Holocaust; buried in Karlin cemetery.

Sir Israel Brodie.event: Appointed Rabbi of Melbourne Hebrew Congregation, Australia.
Helped establish the Zionist Federation of Australia.
Returned to Britain; continued rabbinic and communal leadership.
Evacuated from Dunkirk during World War II.
Concluded service as Senior Jewish Chaplain of the British Forces (Forces Rabbi).
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Great Britain and the Commonwealth.
Founded the Conference of European Rabbis to rebuild Jewish life after the Holocaust.
Banned Rabbi Dr. Louis Jacobs from becoming Principal of Jews' College, sparking a major religious controversy.
Retired as Chief Rabbi and was knighted 'for services to British Jewry'.

Rabbi Chaim ben Attar (the Elder).event: Rabbi Chaim ben Attar (the Elder) establishes himself as Rosh Yeshiva in Salé, Morocco, where the synagogue becomes known as the 'Ben Attar Synagogue.'
Following the death of his brother Shem Tov, Rabbi Chaim ben Attar travels with his son Moshe and grandson Chaim ibn Attar to Meknes to oversee family business.
Due to Jewish persecution by the local vizier, Rabbi Chaim ben Attar and his family flee Meknes. He resides for about two and a half years with Nagid Rabbi Moshe De Avila.
Rabbi Chaim ben Attar (the Elder) passes away at an advanced age, reportedly over 100 years old. Rabbi Moshe Berdugo delivers his eulogy.

R' Chaim HaLevi Soloveitchik (Rav Chaim.event: Born in Volozhin as the second son of Rabbi Yosef Dov (Beis HaLevi) Soloveitchik and Zirl née Efron.
As a youth, followed his father to Slutsk, where he studied under Rabbi Mendel of Slutsk.
Returned to Volozhin; at age 27 became Rosh Yeshiva as Deputy Netziv.
With the closure of the Volozhin Yeshiva, appointed rabbi of Brisk, where he led the community until his passing.
Passed away in Otwock, Poland; buried in the Jewish cemetery of Warsaw next to his wife's grandfather, the Netziv.

R'Chaim Kanievsky.event: R'Chaim Kanievsky born in Pinsk, Poland, eldest son of Rabbi Yaakov Yisrael Kanievsky (the Steipler) and Pesha Miriam (sister of the Chazon Ish).
R'Chaim Kanievsky immigrates with his family to Mandatory Palestine; settled in Bnei Brak, initially living with the Chazon Ish. He studies in Talmud Torah R'Akiva -Center
R'Chaim Kanievsky studies at Tiferet Tzion Yeshiva under Rabbi Michel Yehuda Lefkowitz.
R'Chaim Kanievsky after a brief stint in the Beit Yosef Yeshivah in B'nei B'Rak he transferred to Lomza Yeshiva in Petah Tikva, learning under Rabbi Reuven Katz and Rabbi Shach.
R'Chaim Kanievsky drafted during the War of Independence to guard the southern front; continued Torah study even during guard duty.
R'Chaim Kanievsky settles permanently in Bnei Brak after his marriage to Batsheva Esther, daughter of Rabbi Yosef Shalom Elyashiv; joined Kollel Chazon Ish and studied intensively for decades.
R'Chaim Kanievsky begins publishing seforim, including 'Tevuna' on the Jerusalem Talmud; gained recognition as a halachic authority.
R'Chaim Kanievsky passes away in Bnei Brak on March 18. Funeral attended by 500,000–750,000 people, one of the largest in Israel’s history.

ר׳ אברהם חיים נאה (Avraham Chaim Naeh).event: Born in Hebron into a family connected to the Chabad community.
Moved to Jerusalem; studied under leading rabbis of the Old Yishuv, gaining a reputation for halachic expertise.
Became dayan and halachic authority of the Chabad community in Jerusalem; authored Kezot HaShulchan clarifying halachic rulings.
Published Shiurei Torah, establishing standardized shiurim (halachic measurements) widely adopted across the Jewish world.
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on Har HaMenuchot. His rulings on shiurim remain a standard reference in halacha.

Chaim Ozer Grodzinski.note: Studied with the perushim in Eishyshok; became bar mitzvah
Studied at Volozhin Yeshiva; joined shiur of Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik
Appointed dayan of Vilna Beth Din after his father-in-law's death
Participated in founding conference of Agudath Israel in Kattowitz
Helped found and led Va'ad ha-Yeshivot in Vilna

חיים שמולביץ (Chaim Shmulevitz).event: Born in Kovno, Lithuania (then Russian Empire).
Entered the Mir Yeshiva; became close student of Rav Eliezer Yehuda Finkel.
Became mashgiach and later Rosh Yeshiva in Mir, developing a reputation for brilliance and warmth.
Led the Mir Yeshiva's wartime exile to Shanghai, sustaining Torah study through WWII.
Immigrated to Jerusalem; reestablished Mir Yeshiva there with Rav Eliezer Yehuda Finkel.
Delivered famed mussar discourses (*sichos*) that inspired generations of students.
Passed away in Jerusalem; tens of thousands attended his funeral.

Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin.event: Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin is born in Volozhin, then part of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.
At age 25, Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin becomes a disciple of the Vilna Gaon, whose penetrating method of Torah study shapes his life and teachings.
Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin founds the Volozhin Yeshiva (Etz Chaim), which becomes the model for Lithuanian yeshivas and the central institution of Torah study in Eastern Europe.
A fire destroys many of Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin’s responsa manuscripts, a great loss to future generations.
Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin passes away in Volozhin. Leadership of the yeshiva is continued by his son Rabbi Yitzchak and later by the Netziv.

ר׳ סיריל קליין האריס (Cyril K. Harris).event: Ordained at Jews’ College, London; began rabbinic career serving congregations across Britain.
Appointed Chief Rabbi of South Africa; became spiritual leader of South African Jewry during the transition from apartheid.
Spoke at Nelson Mandela’s presidential inauguration, representing the South African Jewish community and supporting reconciliation.
Retired from position as Chief Rabbi of South Africa.
Died in Edinburgh, Scotland, and buried in Jerusalem.

Rabbi David ben Hassin.event: Rabbi David ben Hassin is born in the Mellah of Meknes, Morocco.
At age 17, Rabbi David ben Hassin writes his first work Migdal LaDavid, a study on biblical interpretation and methods of Torah learning.
Rabbi David ben Hassin marries the daughter of his teacher, Rabbi Mordechai Birdugo, and continues his Torah and poetic work.
Rabbi David ben Hassin composes piyyutim that spread across Morocco and beyond, with themes of exile, persecution, consolation, and redemption.
Rabbi David ben Hassin passes away in Meknes on the 10th of Tammuz, 5552 (June 30, 1792).
Posthumously, Rabbi David ben Hassin's collection of poems Tehillah LeDavid is published in Amsterdam, spreading his works widely across the Jewish world.

אליהו אליעזר דסלר (Eliyahu Eliezer Dessl.event: Born in Homel (Belarus, then Russian Empire), into a family of Musar scholars.
Entered the Kelm Talmud Torah, founded by R’ Simcha Zissel Ziv, where he absorbed the Musar tradition.
Settled in London and later Manchester, teaching Torah privately and working in business for support.
Appointed mashgiach of Gateshead Yeshiva in England, where he became a major Musar influence on postwar European Jewry.
Moved to Bnei Brak, Israel, serving as mashgiach of the Ponovezh Yeshiva until his death.
Died in Bnei Brak. His teachings were collected and published posthumously as 'Michtav Me’Eliyahu', shaping contemporary Jewish thought.

ר׳ דב לנדו (Dov Landau).event: Born in Jerusalem to a rabbinic family.
Studied in Hebron Yeshiva and Ponevezh; became close to Rav Shach.
Joined Slabodka Yeshiva in Bnei Brak as a maggid shiur.
Became Rosh Yeshiva of Slabodka, molding many thousands of talmidim.
Regarded as one of the Gedolei HaDor after Rav Edelstein’s passing.

ר׳ גרשון אדלשטיין (Gershon Edelstein).event: Studied in Lithuanian cheders and yeshivot with his brother Rav Yaakov Edelstein.
Immigrated to Eretz Yisrael during World War II; joined Ponevezh Yeshiva.
Became close talmid of the Chazon Ish; began teaching in Ponevezh.
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Ponevezh after decades of shiurim.
After Rav Shteinman’s passing, became widely regarded as Gadol Hador.
Passed away in Bnei Brak; massive levaya attended by multitudes.

Elchonon Bunim Wasserman.event: Born in Biržai (Birz), Lithuania, to Naftali Beinish and Sheina Rakhel.
Family moved to Bauska, Latvia. At age 15 entered Telshe Yeshiva, studying under Rav Eliezer Gordon and Rav Shimon Shkop.
During school breaks in Bauska, attended shiurim from Rav Avraham Yitzchak Kook, rabbi of the city.
Studied in Brisk under Rav Chaim Soloveitchik for two years.
Married Michla Atlas, daughter of Rabbi Meir Atlas of Salantai. Lived in Salantai, dedicating years to Torah study.
Founded a mesivta in Mstislavl with Yoel Baranchik, earning reputation as a great teacher.
Joined the Chofetz Chaim’s Kollel Kodshim in Radin; studied intensely with Rav Yosef Shlomo Kahaneman.
Appointed rosh yeshiva in Brest-Litovsk at the urging of the Chofetz Chaim.
World War I scattered the yeshiva. Fled with the Chofetz Chaim and reestablished in Smilavichy, where he became rosh yeshiva.
Settled in Baranovichi, Poland, and became rosh yeshiva of Ohel Torah-Baranovich, which grew to nearly 300 students.
Spent 17 months in the U.S. raising funds and inspiring communities, collecting about $10,000 for his yeshiva.
Returned to Poland despite danger, unwilling to abandon his yeshiva. Criticized American institutions as spiritually weak.
Fled to Vilnius during Nazi invasion, continuing to lead his students in exile.
Arrested in Kaunas with 12 other rabbis. Murdered at the Seventh Fort on July 6, 1941 (11 Tammuz 5701). Urged repentance before death.
Remembered as spiritual heir to the Chofetz Chaim. His lectures circulated widely, and his surviving sons continued his legacy.

Rabbi Eliezer Yehuda Finkel.event: Born in Kelm, Lithuania, to Rabbi Nosson Tzvi Finkel (the Alter of Slabodka).
Family moved to Slobodka, where his father established Yeshivat Knesset Yisrael.
Studied in Brisk under Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik.
Sent by his father to join the new Slutsk Yeshiva under Rabbi Yaakov Dovid Willowski (Ridvaz).
Married Malka, daughter of Rabbi Eliyahu Baruch Kamai, Rosh Yeshiva of Mir.
During WWI, led the Mir Yeshiva in exile in Poltava, Ukraine.
Became Rosh Yeshiva of Mir following the death of his father-in-law Rabbi Eliyahu Baruch Kamai.
Escaped war-torn Europe and settled in Jerusalem, reestablishing the Mir Yeshiva.
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on Har HaMenuchot.

Eliyahu Lopian.event: Studied Mussar in Kelm Talmud Torah under Rabbi Simcha Zissel Ziv
Emigrated; served as mashgiach at Etz Chaim Yeshiva in East End
Moved to Israel; served at Knesses Chizkiyahu Yeshiva
Passed away and was buried on Mount of Olives

Rabbi Yosef Shalom Elyashiv.event: Family immigrated to Jerusalem; studied under leading sages.
Began serving as a dayan in the Jerusalem Rabbinical Court.
Resigned from official court work to dedicate himself fully to learning and halakhic rulings.
Recognized as a global halakhic authority; thousands sought his rulings.
Passed away in Jerusalem; over 600,000 attended his funeral.

Rabbi Joseph ben Emanuel Ergas.event: Rabbi Joseph ben Emanuel Ergas is born in Livorno, Italy, into a rabbinic family with connections to kabbalists and followers of Shabbatai Zevi.
Rabbi Joseph Ergas marries Sarah, who would bear him six children.
Rabbi Joseph Ergas settles in Pisa, where he establishes the 'Noah Shalom' yeshiva and charitable institutions.
The Sabbatean Neḥemiah Ḥayyun arrives in Livorno; Rabbi Ergas refuses to endorse him and expels him from the city, marking him as a staunch opponent of Sabbateanism.
Rabbi Joseph Ergas publishes his polemical works Tokhaḥat Meguleh and Ha-Tzad Naḥash in London, responding to Sabbatean attacks.
Rabbi Joseph ben Emanuel Ergas passes away in Livorno on 3 Sivan 5490, leaving a legacy as a kabbalist and defender of rabbinic tradition.

ישראל יעקב פישר (Yisrael Yaakov Fisher).event: Born in Jerusalem to a prominent rabbinic family.
Became a close disciple of Rav Zelig Reuven Bengis, Av Beis Din of the Edah HaChareidis, and later of Rav Isser Zalman Meltzer.
Appointed dayan (rabbinical judge) in the Beis Din of the Edah HaChareidis, where he became known for clear and forceful halachic rulings.
Rose to become head of the Beis Din Tzedek of the Edah HaChareidis, widely respected across the Haredi world.
Passed away in Jerusalem and was buried on Har HaMenuchot. Left a legacy of Torah rulings and halachic responsa.

Joseph Herman Hertz.event: Born in Rebrín/Rebrény, Kingdom of Hungary (now Slovakia).
Emigrated with family to New York City.
Graduated as the first rabbinical graduate of the Jewish Theological Seminary of America.
Moved to Johannesburg, South Africa, as rabbi of Witwatersrand Old Hebrew Congregation. Advocated Jewish and Catholic civil rights.
Professor of Philosophy at Transvaal University College (later University of the Witwatersrand).
Returned to New York as rabbi of Orach Chayim Congregation.
Elected Chief Rabbi of the United Hebrew Congregations of the British Empire.
Began his 'Imperial tour' — first visiting South Africa (66,000 Jews).
Visited Australia and New Zealand Jewish communities during his Imperial tour.
Concluded Imperial tour in Canada, addressing major communities in Montreal, Toronto, and Halifax.
After his tour, met King George V at Buckingham Palace to report on Jewish communities across the Empire.
Became vocal supporter of Zionism and strong opponent of Reform and Liberal Judaism.
Mobilized worldwide Jewish and Christian opposition to League of Nations calendar reform proposal.
Served as Acting Principal of Jews' College during WWII.
Co-founded the Council of Christians and Jews with Archbishop of Canterbury William Temple.
Died in office and was buried in Willesden United Synagogue Cemetery, London.

יצחק הלוי הרצוג (Yitzchak HaLevi Herzog).event: Born in Łomża, Poland, though raised in Leeds, England.
Became Chief Rabbi of Ireland; known as the 'Sinn Féin Rabbi' for his sympathy with Irish independence.
Succeeded Rav Kook as Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Mandatory Palestine.
Appealed to world leaders to rescue European Jewry during the Holocaust.
Became the first Chief Rabbi of the newly established State of Israel.
Passed away in Jerusalem. Buried on Har HaMenuchot.

שמשון רפאל הירש (Samson Raphael Hirsch).event: Born in Hamburg, then part of Napoleonic France, into a family with rabbinic lineage.
Studied in Mannheim under Rabbi Jacob Ettlinger; received semicha in 1830.
Appointed Landesrabbiner (chief rabbi) of Oldenburg. Wrote Nineteen Letters on Judaism (1836) and Horeb (1838).
Became rabbi of Emden, serving Hanoverian districts Aurich and Osnabrück. Founded a secondary school integrating Torah and secular studies.
Elected rabbi of Nikolsburg, Moravia. In 1847 became chief rabbi of Moravia and Austrian Silesia; active in Landtag advocating Jewish civil rights.
Accepted call to Frankfurt am Main to lead the Orthodox secessionist Israelitische Religionsgesellschaft (IRG). Established Realschule and schools promoting Torah im Derech Eretz.
Founded and edited the journal Jeschurun, publishing his philosophy and commentaries.
Promoted Austritt (secession) after German law permitted Jews to leave Reform-dominated communities; strengthened independent Orthodoxy.
Died in Frankfurt am Main. Buried there; left lasting influence through Torah im Derech Eretz philosophy.

יצחק הוטנר (Yitzchak Hutner).event: Born in Warsaw, Poland, into a rabbinic family.
Studied at the Slabodka Yeshiva in Lithuania under the Alter of Slabodka, absorbing the mussar approach.
Returned to Warsaw and became part of the intellectual Jewish community, publishing Torah articles and essays.
Moved to the United States and began teaching at Yeshiva Rabbi Chaim Berlin in Brooklyn, New York.
Became rosh yeshiva of Yeshiva Rabbi Chaim Berlin, where he developed his unique approach integrating deep philosophical Torah analysis with mussar thought.
Began publishing volumes of 'Pachad Yitzchak', a profound work of Jewish thought structured around the Jewish festivals.
Spent extended periods in Jerusalem, where he influenced Israeli yeshiva students and rabbinic leaders.
Died in Jerusalem. Buried on Har HaMenuchot. Remembered as one of the great Torah thinkers of the modern era.

איסר זלמן מלצר (Isser Zalman Meltzer).event: Born in Mir, Lithuania (then Russian Empire).
Studied in the Volozhin Yeshiva under Rav Naftali Zvi Yehuda Berlin (Netziv) and Rav Chaim Soloveitchik.
Married and became Rosh Yeshiva in Slutsk, establishing a major Lithuanian yeshiva.
Immigrated to Eretz Yisrael; appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Etz Chaim in Jerusalem.
Published *Even HaEzel*, his monumental commentary on the Rambam’s Mishneh Torah.
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on Har HaMenuchot.

Rabbi Aharon Lichtenstein.event: Born in Paris, France, to Yechiel and Bluma Lichtenstein.
Family emigrated to the United States during WWII; he grew up in New York.
Earned B.A. at Yeshiva University; studied at Yeshiva Rabbi Chaim Berlin under Rabbi Yitzchak Hutner and under Rabbi Ahron Soloveichik.
Received M.A. in English Literature at Harvard University.
Completed PhD in English Literature at Harvard under Professor Douglas Bush.
Married Tovah Soloveitchik, daughter of Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik; the couple would have six children.
Served as Rosh Kollel at Yeshiva University’s Rabbi Isaac Elchanan Theological Seminary.
Made aliyah and joined Rabbi Yehuda Amital as Rosh Yeshiva of Yeshivat Har Etzion in Gush Etzion.
Maintained close ties to Yeshiva University as Rosh Kollel of the Gruss Institute in Jerusalem.
Moved to Alon Shvut, where Yeshivat Har Etzion is located.
His son Rabbi Mosheh Lichtenstein appointed co-Rosh Yeshiva alongside him and Rabbi Baruch Gigi, marking generational transition.
Awarded the Israel Prize for Jewish Literature in recognition of his lifelong Torah scholarship and leadership.
Passed away in Jerusalem at age 81; remembered as a leading centrist Orthodox thinker and rosh yeshiva.

אליעזר מלמד (Eliezer Melamed).event: Studied under Rabbi Zvi Yehuda Kook at Yeshivat Mercaz HaRav.
Founded and became Rosh Yeshiva of Yeshivat Har Bracha in the Shomron.
Began publishing the Peninei Halakha series, which would become a cornerstone of Religious Zionist halakhic study.
Peninei Halakha reached widespread popularity; translated into multiple languages.

משה קורדובירו (Ramak).event: Born in Safed, Ottoman Galilee, a city that became a flourishing center of Jewish learning and Kabbalah.
Studied under his brother-in-law Rabbi Shlomo Alkabetz (author of Lecha Dodi) and mastered halakhah and Kabbalah.
Became a disciple of Rabbi Yosef Caro, author of the Shulchan Aruch, while also delving deeply into Kabbalistic texts.
Established himself as the leading Kabbalist of Safed, attracting students from across the Jewish world.
Completed his magnum opus Pardes Rimonim, a systematic exposition of Kabbalah synthesizing earlier traditions.
Died in Safed. At his funeral, the young Arizal declared that a pillar of fire accompanied his bier, symbolizing his saintly stature.

ר׳ משה הלל הירש (Moshe Hillel Hirsch).event: Studied in Mesivta Torah Vodaath under Rav Shraga Feivel Mendelowitz.
Moved to Eretz Yisrael; learned in Chevron Yeshiva.
Became Rosh Yeshiva of Slabodka, Bnei Brak.
Continues to serve as one of the senior Roshei Yeshiva in the Haredi world.

R'Moshe Rosen.event: R'Moshe Rosen born in Brańsk, Grodno Governorate, Poland. He learns in various schools before studying on his own.
R'Moshe Rosen studies in Kovno Kollel and gets Smicha from R'Yizchak Elchanan Spektor and Moshe Danishevsky.
R'Moshe Rosen immigrates to the United States and becomes a teacher at Mesivta Torah v'Daat.
R'Moshe Rosen becomes a Rav in Brooklyn.

Rabbi Moshe Shapira.event: R'Moshe Shapira goes to learn in Ohr Yisrael Yeshivah, Petach Tikvah under R'Yaakov Neiman and R'Yosef Rozovsky
R'Moshe Shapira goes to learn in Ponevezh Yeshivah
R'Moshe Shapira on the advice of the Chazon Ish goes to learn in Yeshivat Knesset Yisrael Chevron in Yerushalayim
R'Moshe Shapira with a group of Bachurim goes to learn in Yeshivas Beis Yehudah under HaRav Yechiel Michel Feinstein
After his marriage to the daughter of HaRav Ahron Bialystotzky, R'Moshe Shapira joins Yeshivat Kaminetz
R'Moshe Shapira joins HaRav Dov Schwartzman in Yeshivas Beis HaTalmud in Bayit Vegan.
R'Moshe Shapira establishes Yeshivas Bais Binyamin in Stamford, Connecticut
R'Moshe Shapira returns to Israel and start to teach and becomes involved in outreach
Establishes Yeshivas Shev Shmaatsa and heads Yeshivat Pischei Olam in Yerushalayim (for Baalei Teshuvah)
Passed away in Jerusalem; tens of thousands attended his funeral.

Rabbi Moshe Soloveichik.event: Born in Volozhin, Belarus, to Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik.
Appointed rabbi of Raseiniai, Lithuania, and dean of a yeshiva.
Moved to Warsaw; became director of Talmud studies at Tachkemoni Seminary.
Immigrated to New York; became Rosh Yeshiva at RIETS, Yeshiva University.
Passed away in New York City; funeral attended by 4,000 mourners.

Rabbi Moshe Feinstein.event: R'Moshe Feinstein moves with his family to Starobin
R'Moshe Feinstein, after a stint in Eitz Chaim Yeshivah goes to study under R'Pesach Pruskin in Kobrin Yeshivah, Shklov
R'Moshe Feinstein moves with the Kobrin Yeshiva to Amacislav
R'Moshe Feinstein becomes Rav of Lyuban
Immigrated to New York City; became Rosh Yeshiva of Mesivtha Tifereth Jerusalem
Passed away in New York City; funeral attended by tens of thousands

Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka.event: Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka is born in Zolkova, Galicia (modern-day Zhovkva, Ukraine).
Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka makes his first aliyah to the Land of Israel with his son Shimshon Chaim, but returns after a short period.
Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka leads the first organized group of Baal Shem Tov’s disciples on a second aliyah to the Land of Israel, eventually settling in Tiberias.
Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka passes away in Tiberias on 2 Tammuz 5525 and is buried in the Baal Shem Tov Students' Plot in the ancient cemetery.

Nathan Marcus HaKohen Adler.event: Received doctorate at University of Erlangen and semikhah from Rabbi Abraham Bing in Würzburg
Appointed Chief Rabbi of the British Empire
Oversaw period of Jewish emancipation in Britain, including Sir David Salomons as first Jewish Lord Mayor of London
Helped establish the United Synagogue by Act of Parliament
Died and buried at Willesden United Synagogue Cemetery, London

אביגדור נבנצאל (Avigdor Nebenzahl).event: Born in New York City, United States.
Immigrated to Israel with his family around the establishment of the State of Israel.
Studied in Jerusalem under Rav Shlomo Zalman Auerbach and other gedolim, becoming known for his sharp halachic mind.
Appointed as a dayan and rabbinic leader in Jerusalem; became recognized for his psak in matters of daily halacha.
Became Chief Rabbi of the Old City of Jerusalem, a position he continues to hold, guiding residents and yeshiva students.
Published works on halacha and Tanach, including acclaimed multi-volume commentaries on the Chumash.

נתן צבי פינקל (Nosson Tzvi Finkel).event: Born in Chicago, Illinois, into a rabbinic family connected to the Slabodka yeshiva legacy.
Moved to Israel at age 15; studied in Mir Yeshiva under Rav Chaim Shmuelevitz.
Became a close talmid of Rav Beinish Finkel and was deeply involved in Mir Yeshiva's growth.
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Mir; expanded the yeshiva into one of the largest Torah institutions in the world.
Despite battling Parkinson’s disease, continued to deliver powerful shiurim and mussar talks to thousands.
Passed away in Jerusalem; tens of thousands attended his levayah, mourning the loss of a giant of Torah.

Rabbi Raphael Berdugo (Archangel Raphael.event: Rabbi Raphael Berdugo is born in Meknes, Morocco, into the famous Berdugo rabbinic dynasty.
At age 15, Rabbi Raphael Berdugo is orphaned. His Torah brilliance is revealed publicly during his father’s memorial.
Rabbi Raphael Berdugo becomes dayan in Meknes alongside his brother Rabbi Yekutiel and Rabbi Baruch Toledano.
After the death of his brother Rabbi Yekutiel, Rabbi Raphael Berdugo becomes head of the Beit Din of Meknes, recognized as one of the generation’s great poskim.
Rabbi Raphael Berdugo enacts communal regulations reducing excessive spending on celebrations, bringing relief to struggling families.
Rabbi Raphael Berdugo dies on Hoshana Rabbah at age 75, after completing study of the Idra from the Zohar. His rulings remain authoritative across Moroccan Jewry.

Rabbi Raphael Hayyim Isaac Carregal.event: Rabbi Raphael Hayyim Isaac Carregal is born in Hebron, Ottoman Empire.
Rabbi Carregal begins his travels, spending two years in Constantinople (1754–1756).
Rabbi Carregal serves in Curaçao (1761–1763), where he appears to have held rabbinic duties.
Rabbi Carregal teaches at the Bet Midrash in London, earning a salary of £100 per year.
Rabbi Carregal spends a year in Jamaica before traveling to North America.
Rabbi Carregal sojourns in Newport, Rhode Island, where he befriends Ezra Stiles of Yale, preaches sermons in Spanish, and publishes the first Jewish sermons printed in America.
Rabbi Carregal arrives in Barbados, where he spends his final years.
Rabbi Raphael Hayyim Isaac Carregal passes away in Barbados on May 5, 1777.

Yisrael Salanter.note: Born in Žagarė, Lithuania (then Russian Empire)
Married Esther Fega Eisenstein; moved to Salant, studied under R. Hirsch Broda and R. Yosef Zundel
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Meile’s Yeshiva (Tomchai Torah) in Vilna
Established independent yeshiva in Zaretcha (suburb of Vilna)
Moved to Kovno; established Musar-focused yeshiva at the Nevyozer Kloiz
Left Lithuania for Prussia; lived with Hirsch brothers in Halberstadt
Edited and published journal 'Tevunah' in Königsberg
Lived for periods in Berlin; continued teaching Musar
Organized Russian Jewish immigrants into a community in Paris
Died in Königsberg (then East Prussia, now Kaliningrad)

Rabbi Sar Shalom Mizraḥi Sharabi (Rashas.event: Rabbi Sar Shalom Sharabi (Rashash) is born in Sharab, Yemen.
Rabbi Sharabi stays in Damascus, where he debates leading sages over the minimum kezayit of matzah.
Rabbi Sharabi arrives in Jerusalem, joining the circle of kabbalists at Beit El Yeshiva and initially working in humility as a servant.
The Rashash passes away in Jerusalem and is buried on the Mount of Olives. His grave remains a site of pilgrimage for kabbalists.

שלום שכנא מלובלין (Rabbi Shalom Shachna.event: Born in Kraków, Poland, into a distinguished rabbinic family.
Studied under Rabbi Jacob Pollak in Kraków; absorbed the pilpul method.
Founded a yeshivah in Lublin, which became the most prominent center of Jewish learning in Poland.
Served as rabbi of Lublin and head of its yeshivah; attracted hundreds of students.
Taught Rabbi Moses Isserles (Rema), who later became his son-in-law.
Died in Lublin; buried there. Left a lasting legacy through his yeshivah and disciples.

Rabbi Hershel Schachter.event: Born in Scranton, Pennsylvania, into a rabbinic family; his father, Rabbi Melech Schachter, was a rosh yeshiva at Yeshiva University.
Became assistant to Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik at Yeshiva University at the age of 22.
Received rabbinic ordination and became the youngest rosh yeshiva at RIETS.
Appointed Rosh Kollel at RIETS after Rabbi Aharon Lichtenstein made aliyah to Israel.
Became a halakhic advisor to the Orthodox Union, guiding kashrut and communal issues.
Publicly ruled that reporting abusers to the authorities is permitted and necessary when they are a public danger.
Reiterated in London that mesirah (informing) is permitted in cases of abuse, emphasizing rabbinic/psychological assessment of credibility.
Published responsa during the COVID-19 pandemic guiding Jews on halakhic observance under quarantine, emphasizing both halakhic integrity and public health.

שלמה הלוי אלקבץ (Shlomo HaLevi Alkabetz).event: Born in Salonica (Thessaloniki), Ottoman Greece, into a family of Spanish exiles after the 1492 Expulsion.
Began studies in Talmud and Kabbalah; already recognized as a promising poet and mystic.
Traveled to Jerusalem, where he joined the mystical circles studying Zohar. Influenced by encounters with leading scholars.
Settled in Safed, becoming a leading figure in the emerging school of Kabbalah. Taught Rabbi Moshe Cordovero (the Ramak).
Composed Lecha Dodi, a hymn welcoming the Sabbath Bride, which became part of the universal Jewish liturgy.
Died in Safed, where he was buried alongside other kabbalists. Remembered as a central poet and mystic of the Golden Age of Safed.

שלמה גורן (Shlomo Goren).event: Immigrated to Mandatory Palestine with his family.
Began studying at Hebron Yeshiva in Jerusalem; quickly became known as an ilui (prodigy).
Appointed as the first Chief Rabbi of the Israel Defense Forces during the War of Independence.
Famously blew the shofar at the Western Wall after its liberation in the Six-Day War.
Elected Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel, serving until 1983.
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on Har HaMenuchot.

ר׳ שלמה זלמן אויערבך (Shlomo Zalman Auer.event: Born in the Shaarei Chesed neighborhood of Jerusalem to Rabbi Chaim Yehuda Leib Auerbach.
Studied at Etz Chaim Yeshiva; became a disciple of Rav Isser Zalman Meltzer and other leading sages of Jerusalem.
Published 'Meorei Esh,' the first sefer to systematically address the halachot of electricity.
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Kol Torah in Jerusalem, where he taught thousands of students.
Recognized as one of the greatest halachic decisors of the generation, consulted on medical and technological halacha worldwide.
Passed away in Jerusalem. Over 300,000 people attended his funeral, one of the largest in Israel’s history.

משה שטרנבוך (Moshe Sternbuch).event: Born in London, England, into a rabbinic family.
Studied under Rav Elchonon Wasserman during his stay in London before WWII.
Served as a rabbinic leader in Johannesburg, South Africa, strengthening Torah life and halakhic observance.
Settled in Jerusalem; became dayan and later rosh av beis din of the Edah HaChareidis.
Recognized internationally as a major halakhic decisor, consulted on complex questions in kashrut, medicine, and public policy.

Yitzchak Elchanan Spektor.note: Born in Ros (Rosh), Belarus, Grodno Governorate
Studied in Vilkovisk under R. Benjamin Diskin
First rabbinic position in Sabelin, Belarus
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Kovno, position held until death
Participated in St. Petersburg conferences after anti-Jewish riots

Rabbi Isser Yehuda Unterman.event: Born in Brisk, Russian Empire (today Brest, Belarus)
At age 12 joined Etz Chaim Yeshiva in Maletz under Rabbi Zalman Sender Kahana Shapira
Briefly studied at the Mir Yeshiva, later returning to Maletz to learn under Rabbi Shimon Shkop
Studied at the Volozhin Yeshiva Kollel, ordained by Rabbi Raphael Shapiro
Founded a small yeshiva in Vishnevo and served as rabbi in Mohilev and Mstibovo (Lithuania)
After WWI served as rabbi in Lunovlya, later moving to Grodno
Moved to Liverpool, England; became city rabbi, founded Liverpool Academy for Torah Studies, and built a strong Mizrachi presence
Appointed Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv after Rabbi Moshe Avigdor Amiel’s passing
Elected to the Chief Rabbinate Council in Jerusalem
Elected Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel; served until 1972
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on the Mount of Olives

ר׳ שלמה וולבה (Shlomo Wolbe).event: Born in Berlin, Germany, into a modern Orthodox Jewish family.
Studied at the Hildesheimer Rabbinical Seminary; influenced by German Orthodoxy and the writings of Rav Hirsch.
Joined the Mir Yeshiva in Latvia, immersing himself in mussar and yeshiva life.
Escaped to Sweden during World War II; ministered to Holocaust refugees and published works anonymously.
Settled in Israel; became mashgiach of Yeshivat Be’er Yaakov, building a generation of Torah students.
Founded Yeshivat Givat Shaul in Jerusalem, emphasizing mussar education and personal growth.
Passed away in Jerusalem; buried on Har HaMenuchot. Remembered as the leading mussar master of his generation.

יעקב קמינצקי (Yaakov Kamenetsky).event: Born in Kalushkovo, near Kovno, Lithuania (then Russian Empire).
Studied in the Slabodka Yeshiva under the Alter of Slabodka (Rav Nosson Tzvi Finkel) and later in Slutzk under Rav Isser Zalman Meltzer.
Emigrated to the United States; became rabbi of Tzitevianer Shul in Brooklyn.
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Mesivta Torah Vodaath in Brooklyn; became one of the guiding lights of American Orthodoxy.
Published *Emes L’Yaakov*, his Torah commentary with halachic and hashkafic insights.
Passed away in New York; widely mourned as a fatherly leader of American Jewry.

יעקב פולאק (Rabbi Jacob Pollak).event: Born in Poland (possibly Prague or Kraków). Little is known of his early years.
Settled in Kraków; founded a yeshivah that attracted many students.
Served as chief rabbi of Kraków; pioneered the method of pilpul in Talmud study.
Expelled from Kraków; moved to Vienna and served as rabbi there.
Taught in Vienna; influenced a generation of students including Shalom Shachna of Lublin.
Died in Vienna; remembered as a founder of the Polish–Lithuanian rabbinic tradition.

האדמו״ר מגור (Yaakov Aryeh Alter).event: Born in Jerusalem into the Ger dynasty.
Married daughter of Rav Pinchas Menachem Alter (Pnei Menachem).
Succeeded his uncle, the Pnei Menachem, as Gerrer Rebbe.
Leads Ger Chassidus with tens of thousands of followers worldwide.

ירוחם ליבוביץ (Yerucham Levovitz).event: R' Yerucham Levovitz Born in Lyduokiai/Luban (near Kelm, Lithuania).
R' Yerucham Levovitz goes to study in Slabodka and becomes a talmid of the Altar of Slabodka.

R' Yerucham Levovitz goes to study in Kelm, after 6 years in Slabodka. He describes this period of time in detail in his shiurim and writings. He writes he wept for a whole night when the Altar of Kelm passed away shortly after and he felt his whole understanding came from then. He learns b'chavruta with R'Naftali Trop for 10 years.
R' Yerucham Levovitz goes to Uzvant to help his wife running a shop.
R' Yerucham Levovitz is hired as mashgiach for the Radun Yeshivah and becomes very popular for his charisma and shiurim.

R' Yerucham Levovitz leaves the Radun Yeshivah. He does so as there was a risk of him eclipsing the Chofetz Chaim, and out of his immense respect for the Chafetz Chaim he leaves. His Rebbeim in Kelm encourage him to take a position in Mir Yeshivah which also had recently become a mussar style Yeshivah.
R' Yerucham Levovitz moves with the Mir Yeshivah to Poltava. His family remains in Uzvant.

R' Yerucham Levovitz crosses at some point back to visit his family (on the German side). Sometime around this he meets the Altar of Novardok and they have a famous conversation. He is unable to return however and does not return to the Yeshiva for several years.
R' Yerucham Levovitz becomes Mashgiach in Ponovezh Yeshivah.
R' Yerucham Levovitz becomes Mashgiach in Slabodka building a new Yeshivah in the wake of the first world war chaos.

R' Yerucham Levovitz returns to Kelm to support the Yeshivah there. He debated there with R'Reuvan Dov Dessler whether Kelm should be kept elite or whether they should bring down the message to the lower level. R' Yerucham Levovitz moves on because of this debate.

R' Yerucham Levovitz returns to the Mir and tries to give the ideas he had learnt from Kelm and his wanderings to the students in the last 12 years of his life. These are often referred to as the golden age of the Mir Yeshiva.
R' Yerucham Levovitz passed away in Mir, Belarus.

Rabbi Yisrael Meir Lau.event: Liberated from Buchenwald concentration camp as a child survivor.
Immigrated to Eretz Yisrael; began Torah studies in yeshivot.
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv.
Became Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel (served until 2003).
Returned as Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv.

האדמו״ר מבעלז (Yissachar Dov Rokeach).event: Born in Tel Aviv shortly after the Holocaust.
Appointed as Belzer Rebbe while still a teenager.
Oversaw construction of the great Belz Beis Medrash in Jerusalem.
Continues to lead the Belz community with global institutions.

יוסף קארו (Rabbi Yosef Karo).event: Born in Spain or Portugal, shortly before the expulsion of Jews from Iberia.
Exiled with his family following the expulsion of Jews from Spain and Portugal. Settled in the Ottoman Empire.
Lived in Nikopol (in present-day Bulgaria, then Ottoman Empire), where he began writing his commentary on the Tur, Beit Yosef.
Settled in Safed, Galilee. Became head of the rabbinic court, working alongside Rabbi Moshe of Trani and later Rabbi Moshe Cordovero and the Ari (Rabbi Isaac Luria).
Published the Shulchan Aruch in Venice, which together with Rabbi Moshe Isserles' glosses became the standard halakhic code.
Died in Safed, where he is buried. Revered as one of the greatest codifiers in Jewish history.

Rabbi Yosef Shlomo Kahaneman.event: Born in Kuliai, Kovno Governorate, Russian Empire
Studied at Telshe Yeshiva under Rabbis Eliezer Gordon and Shimon Shkop
Studied at Raduń Yeshiva under the Chofetz Chaim and Rabbi Naftoli Trop
Became Rabbi of Vidzh after marrying the rabbi’s daughter
Appointed Rabbi of Ponevezh; built yeshivas, schools, and an orphanage
Trapped in Palestine during WWII; learned of destruction of Ponevezh Yeshiva and community
Re-established Ponevezh Yeshiva in Bnei Brak; built Kiryat Ha-Yeshiva and orphanages
Declared Israel’s Six Day War victories 'obvious miracles'

Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn.event: Born in Lyubavichi, Russian Empire, only son of Rabbi Sholom Dovber Schneersohn (the Rashab).
At age 15, became secretary to his father and represented him at major Jewish conferences.
Married Nechama Dina Schneersohn, his second cousin and granddaughter of the Tzemach Tzedek.
Appointed head of Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network.
Organized kosher food and supplies for Jewish conscripts during the Russo-Japanese War.
Traveled to Germany and the Netherlands to persuade leaders to intervene against pogroms.
Between 1902–1911, arrested four times by Czarist police for Jewish activism.
Succeeded his father, becoming the sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe.
Expelled from Rostov by Soviet authorities; settled in Leningrad.
Arrested by Soviet secret police, sentenced to death; commuted to exile in Kostroma, then released under international pressure. Departed for Riga.
Visited Palestine, then toured the U.S. to raise awareness for Soviet Jewry; met President Herbert Hoover.
Settled in Warsaw, continued leadership and yeshiva activities until WWII.
Rescued from Nazi-occupied Warsaw through U.S. diplomatic intervention. Arrived in New York on March 19.
Established Lubavitch headquarters in Brooklyn; declared 'America is no different' and founded yeshivot and schools.
Arranged U.S. visa for his son-in-law, Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, rescuing him from Nazi-occupied France.
Founded Kfar Chabad in Israel as a Chabad village near Tel Aviv.
Died January 28 in Brooklyn; buried at Montefiore Cemetery in Queens, later known as 'The Ohel.'
Succeeded by his son-in-law, Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, as the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe.

R' Yoshiyahu Pinto.event: R' Yoshiyahu Pinto is born in Damascus, Syria.
R' Yoshiyahu Pinto moves to Safed intending to settle, but soon suffers the death of his young son Joseph.
R' Yoshiyahu Pinto returns to Damascus after the loss of his son Joseph.
R' Yoshiyahu Pinto passes away in Damascus in February–March 1648 while serving as rabbi of the city.

R'Yitzchok Zilberstein.event: R' Yitzchak Zilberstein born in Bendin, Poland.
Around this time, R' Yitzchak Zilberstein moves with his family to Palestine and he soon goes to learn in Etz Chaim Yeshivah in Yerushalayim under R'Aryeh Levin .
R' Yitzchak Zilberstein goes to study in Slabodka Yeshivah under R'Yechezkel Abramsky who gives him Smicha.

While studying in Slabodka Yeshivah, R' Yitzchak Zilberstein marries the daughter of R'Elyashiv and continues his study in Slobodka Kollel in B'nei B'Rak where he receives another Smicha from R'Wosner.
Around this time, R' Yitzchak Zilberstein becomes Rosh Mesivta and Maggid Shiur in Lucerne Yeshiva.
Around this time, R' Yitzchak Zilberstein returns to Israel and heads the Beit David Insititution in Holon.
R' Yitzchak Zilberstein is appointed Rav of Ramat Elchanan, Bnei Brak.

R' Yitzchak Zilberstein remarries after the death of his wife and mvoes to Ramat Gan. He also joins a Bet Din specially convened to study the effects of the internet on orthodox families. They issued a daat Torah against the use of the computer for entertainment rather than for business.
R' Yitzchak Zilberstein is a member of a committee to investigate conversion and civil marriage in Israel to give recommendations to R'Elyashiv prior to his p'sak.

Rabbi Zvi Hirsch (Hirschel) Levin (Berli.event: Rabbi Zvi Hirsch Levin is born in Rzeszów (Risha), Galicia, into a rabbinic family descended from the Chacham Tzvi.
Rabbi Levin is appointed Rabbi of the Great Synagogue of London, where he is known as Hart Lyon.
Rabbi Levin becomes Chief Rabbi of Berlin, navigating tensions between rabbinic authority and the Jewish Enlightenment (Haskalah).
Rabbi Levin endorses Moses Mendelssohn’s Pentateuch translation and commissions him to write Ritualgesetze der Juden for the government.
Rabbi Levin becomes embroiled in the controversy over Naftali Hertz Wessely’s educational reforms; he briefly resigns his post but later returns.
Rabbi Zvi Hirsch Levin passes away in Berlin after 28 years of service as Chief Rabbi.

Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, Baron Sacks.event: Studys at Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge
Appointed Rabbi of Golders Green Synagogue, London
Appointed Rabbi of Western Marble Arch Synagogue
Installed as Chief Rabbi of the United Hebrew Congregations of the Commonwealth
Elevated to the House of Lords as Baron Sacks of Aldgate

דוד בן שלמה אבן זמרא (Radvaz).event: Born in Spain, possibly Seville, shortly before the expulsion of 1492.
Expelled from Spain; family settled in North Africa, likely in Algiers.
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Egypt (Cairo). Served for nearly 40 years, leading Egyptian Jewry.
Migrated to Safed in the Land of Israel. Joined the circle of great sages including Rabbi Yosef Karo, the Ari, and Rabbi Moshe Cordovero.
Died in Safed; buried there. His thousands of responsa continue to influence halakhic rulings.

רמב״ם.event: Born in Córdoba, Almoravid Spain, into a rabbinic family led by his father Maimon ben Yosef. Córdoba at the time was one of the largest and most affluent cities in Europe. Jews had been here since Roman times. The Rambam's family lived in the south-west part of the city (the Jewish quarter). The Mosque and the Palace were very nearby. The Jewish quarter was narrow and crowded. The Rambam has a younger brother and at least two sisters.

Family fled Córdoba after Almohad conquest abolished Jewish dhimmi status; forced into exile. The Almohads were a fanatical messianic Muslim movement. They united North Africa and Spain under a single Empire and they gave the Jews the option of: exile, conversion or death. Many Jews faked conversion and become Anusim. The Rambam family then wander in Spain for the next 10 years. There is indications that he spent some time in Seville and also in the North of Spain near the land of the Franks.
The Rambam writes a book of logic.

The Rambam writes a book on the Calendar (Maamar HaIbur). This calculated the seasons and the years. He has a strong grasp on astronomy and mathematics. He had already written that he had already studied astrology. He also says he had also studied the entire subject of Idolatry. He wanted to understand the parameters of Avodah Zara.

Settled in Fez, Morocco; studied under Rabbi Yehuda Ha-Cohen Ibn Susan; There is a discussion as to why he settled there considering it was under the same rulership as Cordoba. He wrote parts of his Mishnah Commentary. While in Fez he studies medicine in the University there. While there he tried to find the cause of an accidental overdose given to a ruler.

Writes Igerret HaShmad - What Jews should do now that they are facing forced conversion to Islam. It was written in response to a widely circulated responsa by another Rabbinic figure who said they should lay down their lives rather than conversion. The Rambam disagreed because Islam is monotheistic (he extensively discusses this). He says 1) Accept Islam temporarily to avoid being killed. 2) Keep what Mizvot you can. 3) Move away as quickly as you can as one cannot live their lives as crypto Jews. He notes that even though dying a martyrs death is meritorious, their children would be taken to be Muslims. He goes on to say they should daven in a Mosque. He says that one of the strongest links with man and Hashem is through prayer and one can pray in Arabic. We know that Jews did this and davened in Mosques reciting the Shema.

Joruneys to Israel. The Ship nearly gets destroyed in a storm and the Rambam makes a Neder to fast on those two days and all his descendants if he survives. Sojourn in Acre (Eretz Yisrael) - this is a crusader town and almost a Christian city - nearly all the Mosques had been converted to Churches; visited Jerusalem and prayed on the Temple Mount

Settled in Fustat (Old Cairo), Egypt; became community leader and dayan. There are around 4000 Jews in Cairo with 3 communities. The Karaites (more affluent) and two Rabbinate communites (Iraqi Babylonians and Eretz Yisrael Palesitinians). Their shuls were on the same street. They were both near the Rambam's house. The Shuls were the site of the Bet Din and in general the community centre.
Helped ransom Jewish captives taken during Crusader attack on Bilbeis; began emerging as a leader of Egyptian Jewry.
Wrote Epistle to Yemen, strengthening Jews under persecution
Following his brother David’s death, took up medicine as livelihood
Appointed court physician to Saladin and the Ayyubid court
Passed away in Fustat, Egypt. Buried in Tiberias, Galilee; tomb became a major pilgrimage site

רמב״ן.event: Born in Girona, Catalonia; studied under Judah ben Yakar and Nathan ben Meir; learned Kabbalah from Azriel of Gerona
Began writing halachic works; authored Milhamot Hashem defending Alfasi against Zerachiah ha-Levi
Participated in Disputation of Barcelona against Pablo Christiani before King James I of Aragon
Exiled from Spain; arrived in Jerusalem and re-established Jewish community; founded Ramban Synagogue
Settled in Acre (Akko); gathered students, spread Torah learning, and completed his Torah commentary
Died in the Land of Israel; burial traditions vary (Acre, Haifa, Hebron, or Jerusalem)

Rabbi Moshe Chaim Luzzatto (Ramchal).event: Moshe Chaim Luzzatto is born in Padua, Republic of Venice.
Moshe Chaim Luzzatto publishes his first major literary work, Leshon Limmudim, a treatise on Hebrew style and poetry.
Moshe Chaim Luzzatto leaves Italy under rabbinic pressure and settles in Amsterdam, where he writes his greatest works including Mesillat Yesharim and Derech Hashem.
Moshe Chaim Luzzatto immigrates to the Land of Israel and settles in Acre.
Moshe Chaim Luzzatto and his family die in a plague in Acre on 26 Iyar (16 May 1746).

רשב״א.event: Born in Barcelona, Crown of Aragon; studied under Nachmanides and Yonah Gerondi
Issued responsa to Moravia, earliest evidence of Jewish presence in Austerlitz and Třešť
Documented as banker and communal leader in Barcelona
