Became chief rabbi of Barcelona’s Main Synagogue; widely recognized as authority for Spanish and European Jewry
With Barcelona rabbinate, issued ban forbidding study of philosophy/metaphysics before age 30 (except medicine)
Died in Barcelona; left major halachic and Talmudic works and over 3,000 responsa

רש״י (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzhaki, Rashi).event: Born in Troyes, County of Champagne, France; only child of Yitzhak
At age 17, married and traveled to Worms, Germany; studied under Rabbi Yaakov ben Yakar at the Worms Yeshiva
After Rabbi Yaakov ben Yakar’s death, continued studies in Worms under Isaac ben Eliezer Halevi
Moved to Mainz; studied under Isaac ben Judah, one of the leading sages of the Lorraine region
Returned to Troyes; joined the Beth Din (rabbinical court), began answering halakhic questions
Founded a yeshiva in Troyes, attracting many disciples; began writing his commentaries on Tanakh and Talmud
Witnessed devastation of the First Crusade massacres in Worms and Mainz; composed Selichot mourning the Jewish victims and destroyed yeshivot
Died in Troyes at age 65; buried in the Jewish cemetery of Troyes (later lost, rediscovered by scholars)

Rabbi Baruch Ber Leibowitz.event: Born in Slutsk (near Vilna region, present-day Belarus/Lithuania).
Became a student of Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik in Volozhin Yeshiva.
Served as rosh yeshiva in Halusk and later in Vilna.
Established the Kamenitz Yeshiva after World War I.
Passed away in Vilna just before World War II; his yeshiva students were later murdered in the Holocaust.

Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook.event: Studied at the Volozhin Yeshiva under the Netziv and Rav Chaim Soloveitchik.
Immigrated to Ottoman Palestine; became rabbi of Jaffa and the new agricultural settlements.
Traveled to Europe; became stranded in London during World War I, serving as rabbi there.
Returned to Eretz Yisrael; appointed Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Jerusalem.
Appointed first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Mandatory Palestine.
Passed away in Jerusalem; over 20,000 attended his funeral.

Rabbi Ovadia Yosef.event: Immigrated to Jerusalem with his family.
Served as rabbi and dayan in Cairo, Egypt.
Returned to Israel; became dayan in Petach Tikvah and Jerusalem.
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv.
Elected Rishon LeZion (Sephardic Chief Rabbi of Israel).
Founded the Shas party, transforming Israeli politics and Sephardic representation.
Passed away in Jerusalem; hundreds of thousands attended his funeral.

ר' שמעון גרשון רוזנברג (Rav Shagar).event: Studied at Yeshivat Kerem B’Yavneh; later served in the IDF and was seriously wounded during the Yom Kippur War.
Began teaching at Yeshivat HaKibbutz HaDati and other frameworks, emphasizing Torah study with philosophical depth.
Co-founded Yeshivat Siach Yitzchak in Efrat with Rav Yair Dreifuss, fostering a blend of Hasidic spirituality, postmodernism, and Religious-Zionist learning.
Passed away in Israel, leaving a profound intellectual and spiritual legacy. His writings continue to influence Religious-Zionist and broader Jewish thought.

משה איסרליש (Rema).event: Born in Kraków, Poland, into a prominent rabbinic family.
Established a famous yeshiva in Kraków, attracting students from across Poland and beyond.
Wrote major halakhic works including responsa, Torat HaChatat, and glosses to Shulchan Aruch (HaMapah).
Died in Kraków. Buried in the Rema Cemetery near his synagogue, which still bears his name.

Joseph Rosen.event: Born in Rogachov (Rahachow, Belarus) into a Chabad-Kapust Hasidic family; educated in local cheder.
At age 13, studied under Rabbi Yosef Dov Soloveitchik (Beis Halevi) in Slutsk alongside Chaim Soloveitchik.
Continued studies in Shklov under Rabbi Yehoshua Leib Diskin (Maharil Diskin).
Appointed rabbi of the Hasidic community in Dvinsk (Daugavpils, Latvia), serving nearly 50 years. His counterpart was Rabbi Meir Simcha of Dvinsk.
Gained reputation as one of the greatest halachic authorities of the era, writing responsa rooted in Rambam and Rishonim.
Granted semicha (ordination) to leading rabbis across Europe and America, including future figures such as Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson.
Began publishing his magnum opus, 'Tzofnath Paneach', on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah, along with responsa volumes.
Died in Vienna on 5 March 1936 at a sanatorium; body transported to Dvinsk for burial.

Joel Teitelbaum.event: Born on 13 January 1887 in Máramarossziget (Sighet), Hungary (now Romania), the youngest child of Grand Rabbi Chananyah Yom Tov Lipa Teitelbaum.
Married Chavah Horowitz, daughter of Rabbi Abraham Chaim Horowitz of Połaniec. They had three daughters, all of whom predeceased him.
Settled in Satmar (Szatmárnémeti) and opened a study hall, beginning to attract followers and later styling himself as the 'Rebbe of Satmar.'
Became rabbi of Ilosva (Irshava, Ukraine), establishing a local seminary and spreading Hasidism.
Appointed Chief Rabbi of Krula, serving until moving toward leadership in Satmar.
After years of disputes and elections, officially became Chief Rabbi of Satmar. His yeshiva grew into the largest in the city.
During a royal visit, King Carol II of Romania greeted Teitelbaum first, remarking on his spiritual presence.
After the death of his first wife, married Alte Faige Shapiro of Częstochowa, who later became a powerful force in Satmar leadership.
Deported to the Kolozsvár Ghetto, then placed on the Kastner train. Held in Bergen-Belsen before being released to Switzerland.
Arrived in Mandatory Palestine in September 1945, residing briefly in Jerusalem.
Moved to Williamsburg, Brooklyn, with a small group of Hasidim, laying foundations of the Satmar community in America.
His followers established Congregation Yetev Lev Satmar in Brooklyn, where he became supreme spiritual authority.
Appointed President of the anti-Zionist Edah HaChareidis in Jerusalem; became Chief Rabbi of its Rabbinical Court in 1953.
Founded the Central Rabbinical Congress, uniting anti-Zionist rabbinic voices under his leadership.
Suffered a debilitating stroke that left him partially paralyzed. His wife Alte Faige became a key figure in Satmar affairs thereafter.
Followers founded Kiryas Joel, a self-contained Satmar community in upstate New York, named in his honor.
Died on 19 August 1979 in New York. Over 100,000 attended his funeral in Kiryas Joel. Succeeded by his nephew, Rabbi Moshe Teitelbaum.

Yehudah Aryeh Leib Alter.event: Born in Warsaw, Poland; orphaned young and raised by his grandfather, Rabbi Yitzchak Meir Alter (Chiddushei Harim).
At age 10, visited the Kotzker Rebbe with his grandfather, leaving a deep spiritual impression.
Married Yocheved Rivka, daughter of Yehuda Leib Kaminer. Changed his name to Yehudah Aryeh Leib to avoid duplication.
Declined to become Rebbe after the death of his grandfather. Leadership passed to Rabbi Chanokh Heynekh Levin of Aleksandrów.
Accepted leadership of Ger Hasidim after Rabbi Chanokh Heynekh's death. Began teaching Torah discourses that became the 'Sfas Emes.'
Suffered personal loss with the passing of his wife. A year later, a fire destroyed his home and Beit Midrash. Later remarried Reizel, daughter of Rabbi Baruch Halberstam.
During the Russo-Japanese War, corresponded with Hasidim drafted into the army, encouraging their spiritual growth.
Passed away on January 11 (5 Shevat 5665) at age 57. Funeral drew around 20,000 people despite overcrowded trains. Buried in Ger.
Succeeded by his son Rabbi Avraham Mordechai Alter (Imrei Emes). Some Hasidim followed his brother-in-law Rabbi Pinchas Menachem Justman of Piltz.
Teachings published posthumously as 'Sfas Emes,' which became a cornerstone of Hasidic thought.

עובדיה ספורנו (Ovadiah Sforno).event: Born in Cesena, Italy, into a distinguished Jewish family.
Settled in Bologna, where he served as rabbi and physician, gaining reputation for both Torah scholarship and medicine.
Began writing his Torah commentary, noted for its clarity, rationalism, and moral insight.
Published 'Or Ammim', a philosophical treatise defending Judaism and rational theology.
Died in Bologna; his Torah commentary later became a classic, printed in nearly all editions of Mikraot Gedolot.

Elazar Menachem Man Shach.event: Stayed with Rabbi Chaim Ozer Grodzinski before emigrating
Immigrated to Palestine; served as rosh yeshiva
Appointed rosh yeshiva at Ponevezh Yeshiva
Co-founded Shas political party with Rabbi Ovadia Yosef
Founded Degel HaTorah party after split with Shas and Agudat Yisrael
Passed away; funeral attended by hundreds of thousands

שבתי בן מאיר הכהן (Shabbatai ben Meir Ha.event: Born either in Amstibovo or Wilno (Vilnius), Lithuania.
Entered the yeshivah of Rabbi Joshua Höschel ben Joseph in Tykotzin.
Studied in Kraków under Naphtali Cohen.
Studied in Lublin under Rabbi Ya'acov ben Ephraim Hersch.
Published his magnum opus Siftei Kohen (Shakh) in Kraków.
Returned to Wilno (Vilnius), joined Beit Din under Rabbi Moses Lima.
Fled Wilno during the Northern War; stayed briefly in Lublin.
Resided in Prague after fleeing the war.
Died in Holešov, where his grave is still a pilgrimage site.

Samuel David Luzzatto (Shadal).event: Born in Trieste, Italy (1 Elul 5560). Studied Talmud under Rabbi Abraham Eliezer ha-Levi, and languages and sciences with Mordechai de Cologna, Leon Vita Saraval, and Raphael Baruch Segré.
At age 11, won a prize copy of Montesquieu’s Considérations, sparking his critical thinking. Began writing Hebrew grammar in Italian and exegetical notes on the Torah.
Mother died; took over housework and helped his father’s turning trade while continuing studies and writing poetry (later collected in 'Kinnor Na’im').
Completed Ma'amar ha-Niqqud, a treatise on Hebrew vowels. Advanced in Biblical philology and exegetics.
Began writing 'Torah Nidreshet,' a philosophico-theological treatise, parts of which were later published in Hebrew and Italian.
Father died. Supported himself through private teaching and contributing to Hebrew journals such as Bikkure ha-Ittim.
Appointed professor at the Rabbinical College of Padua. Began his long career of teaching Bible and Hebrew philology.
Expanded literary work: critical studies in Hebrew, Italian, German, and French. Corresponded widely with European Jewish scholars.
Focused research on Biblical philology, Syriac, and Samaritan Hebrew. Critically emended Biblical texts and argued for the authenticity of Isaiah’s full book.
Published works opposing philosophical Judaism and rationalism; attacked Maimonides’ Aristotelianism, Ibn Ezra’s method, and Spinoza’s heresies.
Died in Padua on Yom Kippur (10 Tishrei 5626) after a career of more than 50 years in scholarship and teaching.

בצלאל אשכנזי (Shita Mekubetzet).event: Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a family of Ashkenazi origin that had settled in the Ottoman Empire.
Studied under Rabbi David ibn Abi Zimra (Radbaz) in Egypt, acquiring mastery in halakha and Talmud.
Moved to Safed, where he became part of the flourishing circle of Kabbalists and scholars. Counted among his students were the Arizal and Rabbi Chaim Vital.
Compiled the Shita Mekubetzet, collecting and preserving fragments of Rishonim's commentaries on the Talmud that might otherwise have been lost.
Died in Safed. His Shita Mekubetzet remains an indispensable tool for Talmudic study.

יצחק מקורבייל (Sefer Mitzvot Katan – Sma.event: Born in Corbeil, France, into a prominent rabbinic family.
Active in French rabbinic circles; influenced by Tosafist methodology and the Semag.
Compiled the Sefer Mitzvot Katan (Smak), a condensed, practical halakhic code based on the Semag.
Died in Corbeil, France. His Smak remained a standard halakhic text for centuries in Ashkenazic yeshivot.

דוד הלוי סגל (Taz – Turei Zahav).event: Studied under Rabbi Shlomo Ephraim of Luntschitz (the Keli Yakar) and later became son-in-law of Rabbi Joel Sirkis (the Bach).
Served as rabbi in various communities in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth; began writing halakhic responsa.
Appointed rabbi of Lwów (Lviv). Completed much of his Turei Zahav commentary.
Fled during the Khmelnytsky massacres; lost many family members and disciples.
Settled in Lublin for several years; continued rabbinic leadership and writing.
Died in Lwów. Buried there; remembered as one of the greatest halakhic authorities of his era.

יעקב בן אשר (Ba'al HaTurim).event: Born in Cologne, Holy Roman Empire, during his father’s wanderings (or possibly in Spain).
Moved with his father, the Rosh, to Toledo, Castile, after expulsion of Jews from parts of Germany.
Settled in Toledo. Became a leading posek (decisor), developing the structure of his code, the Arba'ah Turim.
Died in Toledo, Castile. Buried there near his father, the Rosh.

האדמו״ר מויז׳ניץ (Yisroel Hager).event: Born in Bnei Brak to the Vizhnitz Rebbe, the Yeshuos Moshe.
Married into another prominent Chassidic family; groomed for leadership.
Became Rebbe of Vizhnitz in Bnei Brak following his father’s passing.
Leads Vizhnitz institutions and global network of chassidim.

Yaakov Tzvi Mecklenburg.event: Born in Lissa (Leszno), Province of Posen, Germany, into the family of Rabbi Gamliel.
Studied Torah under Rabbi Zechariah Mendel of Lissa, a close friend of Rabbi Akiva Eger.
Began independent Torah scholarship and business career.
After business difficulties, accepted position as Rabbi of Königsberg, East Prussia.
Together with the Malbim, opposed the Reform Judaism convention in Braunschweig.
Published Haketav Vehakkabbalah, his Torah commentary, in Königsberg.
Passed away in Königsberg after serving 34 years as Rabbi. Requested that no eulogies be given at his funeral.

ר׳ יחיאל יעקב וויינברג (Yechiel Yaakov W.event: Born in Ivie, Lithuania (then Russian Empire).
Studied in Slabodka Yeshiva under the Alter of Slabodka and became close with leading Lithuanian Torah figures.
Served as dayan in Vilna, earning reputation for halachic brilliance.
Appointed Rosh Yeshiva of Hildesheimer Rabbinical Seminary in Berlin, teaching a generation of European rabbis.
Arrested by Nazis and imprisoned; later released through international intervention. Survived the war in Switzerland.
Settled in Montreux, Switzerland, where he became a central halachic authority for post-Holocaust European Jewry.
Passed away in Montreux, Switzerland. Buried in Jerusalem; his responsa Seridei Eish became a cornerstone of 20th-century halachic thought.

ר׳ אליעזר יהודה וולְדֶנברג (Eliezer Yehu.event: Born in Jerusalem under Ottoman rule into a rabbinic family.
Studied in Jerusalem yeshivot and became recognized for halachic brilliance at a young age.
Appointed dayan (rabbinical judge) in Jerusalem, beginning his long career in halachic leadership.
Joined the Supreme Rabbinical Court of Israel (Beit Din HaGadol), becoming a senior halachic decisor.
Published early volumes of his responsa Tzitz Eliezer, focusing on contemporary halachic and medical dilemmas.
Issued landmark rulings on medical ethics, including abortion in cases of severe fetal illness and permissibility of organ transplants under Jewish law.
Passed away in Jerusalem at age 91. Buried on Har HaMenuchot. Remembered as a leading posek of medical halacha.

Zvi Hirsch Kalischer.event: Born in Lissa (Leszno), Province of Posen, Prussia. Studied Talmud under Rabbi Jacob of Lissa and Rabbi Akiva Eger.
Settled in Thorn (Toruń), where he spent the rest of his life. Served over 40 years as Rabbinatsverweser (acting rabbi), refusing remuneration and living modestly.
Published 'Eben Bochan' (Krotoschin), commentary on Choshen Mishpat.
Published 'Emunah Yesharah' (Krotoschin), Jewish philosophy and theology, including a commentary on Job and Ecclesiastes.
Published parts I & II of 'Moznayim la-Mishpat' in Krotoschin and Königsberg, commentary on Choshen Mishpat.
Influenced Chaim Lorje to found the first 'Association for the Colonisation of Palestine' in Frankfurt. This was among the earliest proto-Zionist societies.
Published 'Derishat Tzion' in Lyck, calling for Jewish settlement in Israel, agricultural schools, and a Jewish guard force. Advocated self-help and natural redemption.
German translation of 'Derishat Tzion' published in Toruń by Poper. Second Hebrew edition issued in 1866 by N. Friedland.
Due to his influence, the Alliance Israélite Universelle founded the Mikveh Israel agricultural school in Palestine near Jaffa.
Died in Toruń after decades of rabbinic leadership and proto-Zionist advocacy. Remembered as a forerunner of modern Zionism.
